items.correct: Ideological incompatibility plus mutual suspicion after WWII — disputes over Eastern Europe, the atomic bomb, and broken wartime agreements.
items.misconception: Stalin started it by invading Eastern Europe after the war.
items.random_wrong: It began when the USSR shot down a US spy plane.
items.why: Students treat one side as solely responsible. Most historians now see the Cold War as a mutual breakdown — both sides acted on rational fears that the other read as aggression.

items.q: How did the feudal system work in Norman England?
items.correct: The king granted land to tenants-in-chief in return for knight service; they in turn enfeoffed knights who held land in return for military service to their lord.
items.misconception: Feudalism meant peasants were slaves owned by the king.
items.random_wrong: Feudalism was a written constitution introduced by William in 1066.
items.why: Feudalism described a tenurial chain of obligations among free landholders, not slavery. Most peasants were villeins tied to manors — a related but separate system.

items.q: What were the Wars of the Roses really about?
items.correct: Dynastic conflict between rival branches of the Plantagenets (Lancaster and York) over the throne, intensified by weak kingship under Henry VI and noble factionalism.
items.misconception: The whole country was at war for thirty continuous years.
items.random_wrong: They were a religious war between Catholics and Protestants.
items.why: Fighting was sporadic and most of England saw no battles; ordinary life carried on. Students imagine total civil war; in reality, it was an aristocratic dispute fought in short campaigns.

items.q: Who nationalised the Suez Canal in 1956?
items.why: It's Nasser. “Sadat” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many days did the 1967 war last?
items.why: Correct answer: 6. “19” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was the Balfour Declaration issued?
items.why: Exact answer: 1917 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
